coredata.dc:title: Precise spatial control of cavitation erosion in a vessel phantom by using an ultrasonic standing wave
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Aiwei
Huang, Peixuan
Guo, Shifang
Zhao, Lu
Jia, Yingjie
Zong, Yujin
Wan, Mingxi
coredata.dc:description: In atherosclerotic inducement in animal models, the conventionally used balloon injury is invasive, produces excessive vessel injuries at unpredictable locations and is inconvenient in arterioles. Fortunately, cavitation erosion, which plays an important role in therapeutic ultrasound in blood vessels, has the potential to induce atherosclerosis noninvasively at predictable sites. In this study, precise spatial control of cavitation erosion for superficial lesions in a vessel phantom was realised by using an ultrasonic standing wave (USW) with the participation of cavitation nuclei and medium-intensity ultrasound pulses. The superficial vessel erosions were restricted between adjacent pressure nodes, which were 0.87mm apart in the USW field of 1MHz. The erosion positions could be shifted along the vessel by nodal modulation under a submillimetre-scale accuracy without moving the ultrasound transducers. Moreover, the cavitation erosion of the proximal or distal wall could be determined by the types of cavitation nuclei and their corresponding cavitation pulses, i.e., phase-change microbubbles with cavitation pulses of 5MHz and SonoVue microbubbles with cavitation pulses of 1MHz. Effects of acoustic parameters of the cavitation pulses on the cavitation erosions were investigated. The flow conditions in the experiments were considered and discussed. Compared to only using travelling waves, the proposed method in this paper improves the controllability of the cavitation erosion and reduces the erosion depth, providing a more suitable approach for vessel endothelial injury while avoiding haemorrhage.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cavitation
Erosion
Ultrasonic standing wave
Vessel
SonoVue
Phase-change nanoemulsion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cavitation nuclei trapping and erosion controlling
Characteristics of vessel-wall cavitation erosion in the USW
Position shift of vessel-wall cavitation erosion
Position shift of SonoVue microbubble erosion
Position shift of phase-change microbubble erosion
Effects of cavitation pulse parameters on vessel-wall erosion
Effects of cavitation pulse parameters on SonoVue microbubble erosion
Effects of cavitation pulse parameters on phase-change microbubble erosion
Effects of flow velocity on vessel-wall cavitation erosion
Effects of flow velocity on SonoVue microbubble erosion
Effects of flow velocity on phase-change microbubble erosion

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the system setup.

SonoVue microbubble erosion. (a) Erosion of distal vessel wall (marked with blue rectangle) at antinodes in the USW field. (b) Undamaged vessel without USW. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Phase-change microbubble erosion. (a) Erosion of proximal vessel wall (marked with blue rectangle) at an antinode in the USW field. (b) Undamaged vessel without USW. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Enhanced ADV process. (a) Before the emission of cavitation pulse without USW. (b) After the emission of a cavitation pulse without USW. (c) Before the emission of cavitation pulse in the USW field. (d) After the emission of a cavitation pulse in the USW field.

Shift of vessel-wall cavitation erosions by USW modulation. (a) SonoVue microbubble erosion (marked with blue rectangle) before USW modulation. (b) SonoVue microbubble erosion (marked with red rectangle) after USW modulation. (c) Phase-change microbubble erosion (marked with blue rectangle) before USW modulation. (d) Phase-change microbubble erosion (marked with red rectangle) after USW modulation. (e) Shift distances. P1: original erosion position. P2: new erosion position after USW modulation. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Effects of pulse parameters on SonoVue microbubble erosion. (a) Pulse length: 2 cycles. (b) Pulse length: 5 cycles. (c) Pulse length: 8 cycles. (d) Effects of pulse length on erosion size. (e) Effects of pulse repetition frequency on erosion size. (f) Effects of peak negative pressure on erosion size.

Effects of pulse parameters on phase-change microbubble erosion. (a) Pulse length: 10 cycles. (b) Pulse length: 25 cycles. (c) Pulse length: 40 cycles. (d) Effects of pulse length on erosion size. (e) Effects of pulse repetition frequency on erosion size. (f) Effects of peak negative pressure on erosion size.
Effects of flow velocity on SonoVue microbubble erosion.
Effects of flow velocity on phase-change microbubble erosion.

head.ce:title: Precise spatial control of cavitation erosion in a vessel phantom by using an ultrasonic standing wave

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Laboratory of Biomedical Information Engineering of Ministry of Education, Department of Biomedical Engineering, School of Life Science and Technology, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an 710049, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Biomedical Information Engineering of Ministry of Education
School of Life Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In atherosclerotic inducement in animal models, the conventionally used balloon injury is invasive, produces excessive vessel injuries at unpredictable locations and is inconvenient in arterioles. Fortunately, cavitation erosion, which plays an important role in therapeutic ultrasound in blood vessels, has the potential to induce atherosclerosis noninvasively at predictable sites. In this study, precise spatial control of cavitation erosion for superficial lesions in a vessel phantom was realised by using an ultrasonic standing wave (USW) with the participation of cavitation nuclei and medium-intensity ultrasound pulses. The superficial vessel erosions were restricted between adjacent pressure nodes, which were 0.87mm apart in the USW field of 1MHz. The erosion positions could be shifted along the vessel by nodal modulation under a submillimetre-scale accuracy without moving the ultrasound transducers. Moreover, the cavitation erosion of the proximal or distal wall could be determined by the types of cavitation nuclei and their corresponding cavitation pulses, i.e., phase-change microbubbles with cavitation pulses of 5MHz and SonoVue microbubbles with cavitation pulses of 1MHz. Effects of acoustic parameters of the cavitation pulses on the cavitation erosions were investigated. The flow conditions in the experiments were considered and discussed. Compared to only using travelling waves, the proposed method in this paper improves the controllability of the cavitation erosion and reduces the erosion depth, providing a more suitable approach for vessel endothelial injury while avoiding haemorrhage.

ce:section.ce:para: Ultrasound can noninvasively penetrate soft tissues, focusing on a specific site in the human body. The high negative pressure in the focus area can lead to ultrasonic cavitation. Expansions and collapses of the cavitation bubbles produce shockwaves, microstreams, and microjets, leading to structural changes in the fluid-tissue interface [1]. Tissue erosion is one of the most important nonthermal physical phenomena during ultrasonic cavitation.

In blood vessels, cavitation erosion has been explored for therapeutic applications, including thrombolysis, etc. [2,3]. Besides therapeutic usages, cavitation erosion is also a promising method for atherosclerotic inducement in animal models [4]. Currently, mechanical arterial balloon injury combined with hypercholesterolemia is the most widely employed conventional method to induce atherosclerosis in animal models [5]. However, the balloon injury operation is invasive, excessive in arterial endothelial cell damage, and inconvenient in arterioles. In addition, the balloon injures the vessel in uncertain positions, resulting in atherosclerotic plaques developing at unpredictable sites. Cavitation erosion is able to cause endothelial cell damage as well as the balloon injury, providing another possible approach to induce atherosclerosis in animal models. Consequently, improvements in the spatial controllability and precision of cavitation erosion in blood vessels without haemorrhaging may have a significant impact both on the predictability of atherosclerotic locations and on the predictability of atherosclerotic sizes in animal models.

Short high-intensity ultrasound pulses can generate microbubbles at the focus of an ultrasound transducer in both cavitation histotripsy and boiling histotripsy [6,7]. Expansions and collapses of the microbubbles cause mechanical breakdown of a thrombus into acellular debris under high negative pressure [8]. To avoid excessive erosion damage to surrounding vessels and tissues, the number of cycles in one pulse has to be reduced to improve the precision of the cavitation erosion, even reduced to a half-cycle in “microtripsy” with lesion widths are less than a wave length of 3mm. Extremely high negative pressure, between 26.4MPa to 30.0MPa, is required to generate cavitation microbubbles as a result of the cycle reduction [9].

Conventional high-intensity focused ultrasound (HIFU) can erode the proximal vessel wall in a region of more than one centimetre in large blood vessels assisted by microbubble contrast agents. Microbubbles are utilised as cavitation nuclei, which reduce the cavitation pressure threshold and enhance the cavitation effects. The erosion phenomenon occurs under high power, ranging from 50W to 90W. According to Qiao et al., it is difficult to erode the distal wall because of the strong ultrasonic reflection from the crowded cavitation microbubbles [10].

Medium-intensity ultrasound pulses combined with microbubbles are able to induce long and thin tunnels in the distal walls of small blood vessels, increasing the endothelial permeability in drug delivery [11,12]. The peak negative pressure threshold of 1MHz pulses tunnelled in the wall is 2.5MPa under a pulse repetition frequency of 10kHz and a pulse duration of 10μs, and the thin tunnels extend over one millimetre in depth. The pressure threshold decreases while the pulse duty cycle increases [13].

To summarise, though “microtripsy” can produce cavitation erosion precisely, the dependence on special ultrasound transducers and special high-power amplifiers restricts the scenarios in which it can be used. The conventional HIFU combined with microbubble contrast agents is able to erode the proximal vessel walls on a large scale. However, the cavitation erosion that results from HIFU is not precise enough to avoid an excessive lesion, and the irreversible adverse thermal effect also exists as a consequence of the high power. The medium-intensity cavitation pulses assisted by microbubble contrast agents produce long and thin tunnels in the distal walls in small vessels, but these long tunnels cause bleeding and may not be enough to induce atherosclerosis in animal models [14]. Additionally, for atherosclerotic inducement by cavitation erosion, although arterial ruptures and haemorrhages must be avoided, all of these methods produce lesions that mainly extend in the direction of sound propagation, producing injuries too deep in the blood vessel walls. Moreover, microbubble contrast agents are adopted to decrease the pressure threshold of cavitation and enhance erosion. However, microbubble contrast agents distribute in all of the blood vessels because of blood circulation. As a result, excessive erosion during cavitation becomes a potential hazard. Fortunately, ultrasonic standing wave (USW), which has been widely used for concentration, separation, and transportation of microparticles or cells in microfluidics, has been proven to be potentially used to trap microbubble contrast agents at a desired site in a blood vessel, controlling the spatial distribution of the cavitation nuclei [15–18].

The objective of this paper is to provide a method to realise precise spatial control of superficial erosion in a vessel phantom by using an USW with the participation of cavitation nuclei and medium-intensity cavitation pulses. In this method, the cavitation erosion that was generated at the antinode of the USW with submillimetre-scale accuracy could be designated to be in the proximal vessel wall or in the distal vessel wall. The erosion position could be shifted along the vessel by modulating the USW instead of moving the transducers. The shallow erosion holes extended mainly in the vessel axial direction instead of in the direction of sound propagation, hence the term “superficial erosion”. Different erosion geometries were produced under different ultrasonic conditions in the experiments. Effects of the flow conditions on the erosion in the vessel were investigated. The conventional transducers used in this study were easily manufactured. In addition, the medium-intensity cavitation pulses could avoid irreversible adverse thermal effects on blood vessel walls.

In the following sections, the method is introduced in detail. Section 2, methods and materials, presents how the experiments were prepared and conducted in three parts: experimental setup, materials, and control methods. To further describe the advantages of this method, the characteristics of controlled superficial erosion, the position shift of the erosion, the effects of acoustic parameters and the effects of flow velocity on the erosion are introduced and discussed in Section 3.

The experimental setup consists of the USW setup, cavitation setup and high-speed photomicrography setup. The USW setup was used for the erosion control, the cavitation setup was adopted to generate the cavitation erosion, and the high-speed photomicrography setup was the system for observing the experiments. Section 2.2 details the materials, including the blood vessel phantom, cavitation nuclei, and ultrasonic parameter choices depending on the cavitation nuclei. Section 2.3 shows the erosion control methods. The USW regulated the distribution of cavitation nuclei. Hence, the superficial erosion was controlled.

The schematic of the system setup is depicted in Fig. 1 . Two channels of a double-channel arbitrary wave generator (AWG 420, Tectronix, Beaverton, OR, USA) were utilised to generate continuous sinusoidal waves, which were amplified by power amplifiers (AG1016, T&G Power Conversion Inc., Rochester, NY, USA). Two unfocused ultrasonic transducers (1MHz, aperture diameter 30mm, TAPMM25W, Wuxi Lanhui, Wuxi, JS, CN) driven by the power amplifiers were mounted on a water tank with an angle of 120°. Degassed water contained in the water tank, 32cm (long)×24.5cm (high)×10cm (wide), was maintained at 37°C Transmitting powers of the power amplifiers were set at 1W. An USW of 300kPa was generated by the interference of two ultrasonic beams. The transducers were located on the same side of the vessel to avoid potential hindrances, such as acoustic shielding by bones and the space limitations for placing transducers.

Two focused ultrasonic transducers were used separately as the cavitation transducer, driven by a tone burst pulser-receiver system (RPR-4000, Ritec Inc., Warwick, RI, USA) and synchronised with the emission of the USW. The centre frequency of one transducer (aperture diameter 28.575mm, I8-0518-P, Olympus NDT Inc., Waltham, MA, USA) is 5MHz, and the other one (aperture diameter 38.100mm, V392, Olympus NDT Inc., Waltham, MA, USA) has a centre frequency of 1MHz. The frequencies of the cavitation transducers were chosen depending on the cavitation nuclei, which were discussed in Section 2.2.2. The pulse durations were no longer than 8μs, which was in the imaging range, while the peak negative pressures were lower than 1.8MPa. The spatial peak time average sound intensity of the cavitation pulses ranged from 3.8×10−4 W/cm2 to 8.6×10−2 W/cm2. The axes of the transducers in the USW setup, the axis of the vertical cavitation transducer (5MHz or 1MHz), and the axis of the horizontal vessel in the phantom were located in the same plane. The two transducers in the USW setup were placed symmetrically with respect to the vertical cavitation transducer. The focus of the cavitation transducer and the pressure antinode of the USW field overlapped at the vessel in the phantom.

The controllable cavitation erosion of the vessel wall was observed through a long working distance microscope (Questar QM 100, Company Seven Inc., USA) with a magnification ranging from 53.5 diameters to 127 diameters, and recorded by the high-speed photography camera (Motion Pro Y3-S1, Integrated Design Tools Inc., MD, USA). The microscope could work from a distance of 15cm to 35cm. The size of the frames was 1280×1024 pixels, allowing simultaneous observation of the proximal (upper) and distal (lower) vessel walls. The exposure time of the high-speed photography camera was set to be 25μs. Photographs were recorded by the camera before, during, and after the cavitation erosion process at an acquisition frame rate of 50Hz. A higher frame rate of 3000Hz was adopted for a special case, in which the vaporised microbubbles should be captured. The frame rates were high enough to record the slowly changed erosion process and to capture the vaporised microbubbles, as a result, the high-speed imaging did not synchronised with the cavitation pulses.

The transparent vessel phantom made of polyacrylamide hydrogel, which has similar acoustic characteristics with soft tissues, was used to mimic a blood vessel in soft tissue. The 40% (w/v) acrylamide solution was made by 40% (w/v) acrylamide, 1.1% (w/v) N,N′-methylene bis-acrylamide, and deionized water. The gel mix liquid was poured into a rectangular box traversed by a cylinder rod; the box was 12cm (long)×5cm (high)×5cm (wide). After the mixture liquid fully solidified, the cylinder rod was carefully withdrawn to form a vessel with an approximate diameter of 1mm. The solutions were pushed steadily into the vessel by a syringe pump (6010A, Harvard Apparatus, Holliston, MA, USA), with flow velocities ranging from 6.4cm/s to 10.6cm/s. The density, ultrasound speed, and attenuation coefficient in the phantom were 1.003±0.001 (g/cm3), 1512.2±3.0 (m/s), and 0.2±0.01 (dB/cm) [10], respectively. The storage modulus of this phantom was in the range of 2–8kPa, and the loss modulus of this phantom was in the range of 0.4–0.9kPa [10,19].

Two types of widely used cavitation nuclei were adopted in this study: SonoVue and phase-change microbubbles. SonoVue (concentration is 2×108 bubbles/ml and the mean diameter is 2.5μm Bracco SpA, Milan, ITA) is a type of commercially available microbubble ultrasound contrast agent. The gas contained in the microbubbles is sulphur hexafluoride and coated by phospholipid shells. The microbubbles were diluted by undegassed physiological saline. The concentration of the SonoVue used in the experiments was 2×106 bubbles/ml.

The other type of cavitation nuclei was phase-change microbubbles; phase-change microbubbles have resulted in novel ways of approaching diagnostic and therapeutic techniques and have been widely studied by medical researchers [20]. The phase-change microbubbles were produced during an acoustic droplet vaporisation (ADV) process. Phase-change nanodroplet emulsions were prepared by combining degassed deionized water, 3% v/v perfluoropentane (PFP) and 0.8% v/v negatively charged fluorinated surfactant Capstone® FS-63 [21,22]. The PFP density is 1590kg/m3, while the sound phase velocity is closed to 520m/s [23]. The samples were mixed and emulsified with an ultrasonic liquid processor (VC 705, Sonic & Materials, Newton, USA) at an 80% duty cycle for 90s. The initial mean diameter of the phase-change nanodroplets, sized by dynamic light scattering using a Zetasizer nano-sizing system (Malvern, Worcestershire, UK), was 209nm. The concentration of the phase-change nanodroplets was on the order of 1010 droplets/ml. The phase-change nanoemulsions were diluted by undegassed physiological saline under a volume ratio of 1:1500.

ADV is an approach to generate phase-change microbubbles by using ultrasound and phase-change emulsions [24,25]. Ultrasound is used to transit these liquid droplets in the emulsions into gas bubbles, which is known as the ADV process. The phase-change droplets are composed of volatile perfluorocarbons, such as PFP (29°C boiling point). Because of the Laplace pressure caused by surface tension, a nanometre PFP droplet does not spontaneously vaporise when injected in vivo at the temperature of 37°C. However, when the emulsion is exposed to ultrasound above a certain acoustic pressure amplitude, the PFP within the emulsion will be vaporised.

The different types of cavitation nuclei were used to produce cavitation erosions in distinct parts of the vessel wall. The SonoVue microbubbles combined with the 1MHz cavitation pulses were used for the erosion in the distal vessel wall, while the phase-change microbubbles combined with the 5MHz cavitation pulses eroded the proximal vessel wall.

The frequencies of the cavitation pulses were chosen depending on the cavitation nuclei. For the SonoVue microbubble erosion, the threshold for disrupting the vessel wall by lipid-shelled microbubbles increased greatly if the frequency of the cavitation pulses increased from 1MHz to 5MHz [11]. The controlled SonoVue microbubble erosion had the similar result; the pressure threshold of 1MHz cavitation pulses was much lower in the USW field. Thus, the 1MHz ultrasonic pulses were used for SonoVue microbubble erosion. In the phase-change microbubble erosion, the phase-change microbubbles were generated through the ADV process. A higher frequency of cavitation pulses led to a significant decrease of the pressure threshold of the ADV process [22,26]. The experimental results of controlled phase-change microbubbles erosion showed that the pressure threshold of 5MHz cavitation pulses was lower. In addition, the 1MHz cavitation pulses could cause phase-change microbubble erosion simultaneously in both the proximal vessel wall and the distal vessel wall, which should be avoided for the purpose of precise erosion. Consequently, the 5MHz cavitation pulses were chosen for phase-change microbubble erosion.

The double-channel arbitrary waveform generator generated two identical sinusoidal waveform signals synchronously in two channels. The signals were amplified separately by the two power amplifiers with the same amplification factor, and were subsequently applied to the unfocused ultrasonic transducers in the USW setup. The axes of the two transducers in the USW setup and the axis of the horizontal vessel in the phantom were located in the same plane. The angle between the vertical direction and each of the transducer axes was 60°. The two ultrasonic wave beams generated by the transducers met the blood vessel at the same position and at the same time. The USW was generated along the vessel axis by the interference of the two continuous waves with an angle of 120°.

The time averaging ARF was generated from the gradient of the USW pressure field. The primary ARFs would gather the microspheres into the acoustic potential wells, either the pressure nodes or pressure antinodes. In the USW pressure field, the ARFs were calculated according to [27]
(1)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cavitation nuclei trapping and erosion controlling
ce:section.ce:para: According to Eqs. (1)–(3), PFP droplets will assemble in the pressure antinodes in the USW pressure field. A bubble that is larger than the resonance size moves to the nearest node. A sufficiently small microbubble trapped at the nearest antinode [18,28]. The microbubbles attract each other and form clusters under the secondary ARFs, and the convergence of the microbubbles occurs. The converged bubbles and microbubble clusters assemble in the pressure nodes. However, cavitation threshold increment is significant when the microbubble radius increases, resulting in cavitation erosion depending on the small microbubbles that trapped at the antinodes [29]. The ARFs influence the spatial distribution of cavitation nuclei in the USW field. Hence, the positions of cavitation erosions can be regulated.

The nodal (antinodal) positions shift with the relative phase changes of the acoustic waves, whose interference generates the USW. Consequently, the position shift of cavitation erosion can be achieved by modulating nodal positions. The shift distance of the nodal position is expressed as
(4)
D
shift
= λ α / 4 π sin θ
where
λ
is the wave length, α represents the relative phase change, and θ is the half angle between the transducers. In the experiments,
λ
, α, and θ were set to 1.50mm, 0.90π, and 0.33π, respectively. The distance of two adjacent pressure nodes was 0.87mm.

To present the controlled superficial erosion, the method was investigated from four aspects. First, the characteristics of the erosion were explored, and the experimental results showed the superficial erosion generated between adjacent nodes in the proximal vessel wall or in the distal vessel wall. Second, the ability of the USW modulation to shift the position of the erosion was tested. Third, the effects of acoustic parameters of cavitation pulses on the erosion were investigated and discussed. Fourth, the influences of the flow conditions in the vessel were researched and discussed. Because the erosion was dependent on the cavitation nuclei, the experimental results of SonoVue microbubble erosion and phase-change microbubble erosion were given and discussed separately in each part.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of vessel-wall cavitation erosion in the USW
ce:section.ce:para:
The SonoVue microbubbles and the phase-change microbubbles were utilised as cavitation nuclei. Oscillations, ruptures, and jets of the microbubbles, which were regulated by the USW, led to the vessel-wall erosions as shown in Figs. 2 and 3
. The following presents the characteristics of SonoVue microbubble cavitation erosion and the characteristics of phase-change microbubble cavitation erosion separately.
SonoVue microbubble erosion controlled by USW revealed two characteristics: one characteristic was antinodal superficial erosion of the distal wall and the other was a pressure threshold reduction.

Fig.2(a) exhibits the vessel-wall erosion caused by SonoVue microbubbles that were exposed to the 1MHz cavitation pulses in the USW field. The pulse length, repetition frequency and peak negative pressure were 2 cycles, 100Hz, and 1.8MPa, respectively. The duration of the erosion was 150s, and the flow velocity was 6.4cm/s. The erosions arose between pressure nodes of the USW in the distal vessel wall. Four reasons may result in the occurrence of antinodal superficial erosion in the distal vessel wall. First, the cavitation threshold reduction was significant when the microbubble radius decreased, resulting in cavitation erosion depending on small microbubbles [29]. According to Eqs. (1) and (2), small microbubbles were trapped at the nearest pressure antinodes by ARFs; microbubbles larger than the resonance size assembled at the pressure nodes of the USW [18,28]. The violent expansions and collapses of small microbubbles at the antinodes erode the vessel wall while large bubbles at the nodes changed their volumes slightly. Second, the higher effective negative pressure at the antinodes enhanced the cavitation processes as a result of the interference of the USW and the cavitation pulses. Third, the ARFs generated by the USW accelerated the lateral movements of cavitation microbubbles, avoiding the erosion extending too deep as thin tunnels. Fourth, the SonoVue microbubbles were easily pushed to the distal vessel wall; hence, the cavitation occurred close to the distal vessel wall.

The second characteristic was that the USW reduced the pressure threshold of the SonoVue microbubble erosion. The SonoVue microbubble erosion was not captured in Fig.2(b) after withdrawing the USW, although all of the rest of the parameters were set to be the same as in Fig.2(a), including the 1.8MPa cavitation pulses. In the USW field, the negative pressure threshold of cavitation pulses for SonoVue microbubble erosion was 0.9MPa, the peak negative pressures were 1.2MPa at the antinode and 0.9MPa at the node because of the interference with the USW. The effective acoustic pressures combining USW with the cavitation pulses were simulated by MATLAB with Field II package [30,31]. The threshold was confirmed by three repeated experiments. The pressure threshold of cavitation erosion could be reduced resulting from the accumulation of cavitation nuclei and the higher effective negative pressure at the antinodes.

Three Characteristics appeared in the phase-change microbubble erosion in the USW field, including antinodal superficial erosion of the proximal wall, threshold reduction for the erosion, and an ADV process enhancement.

In Fig.3(a), the phase-change microbubbles eroded the proximal vessel wall between two nodes in the USW field during irradiation from the 5MHz cavitation pulses. The pulse length, repetition frequency and peak negative pressure were 40 cycles, 1Hz, and 1.7MPa, respectively. The duration of the erosion was 150s, and the flow velocity was 6.4cm/s. The antinodal superficial erosions occurred in the proximal vessel walls. In addition to the same reasons for antinodal superficial erosion that were described for SonoVue microbubble erosion, there was one more reason for phase-change microbubble antinodal erosion; ADV processes were more likely to occur at the pressure antinode because of the facilitation of heat accumulation at the antinodes of the USW. According to ARF Eq. (3), the phase-change nanodroplets, which were heavy liquid, were hardly pushed entirely to the distal wall. Massive phase-change nanodroplets changed into microbubbles instantaneously during the cavitation pulses, and the acoustic reflection and acoustic shelter from the phase-change microbubble cloud made the distal vessel-wall erosion very difficult. Therefore, the phase-change microbubble erosion arose in the proximal vessel wall at the antinode of the USW.

The second characteristic was the pressure threshold reduction. The cavitation erosion of the vessel wall by phase-change microbubbles did not happen without the USW in Fig.3(b), although all of the rest of the parameters, including the 1.7MPa cavitation pulses, were set to be the same as in Fig.3(a). In the USW field, the pressure threshold of cavitation pulses for phase-change microbubble erosion was 1.2MPa, while peak negative pressures was 1.5MPa at the antinodes and 0.5MPa at the nodes as a result of the interference with the USW. The threshold was confirmed by the three repeated experiments. Three factors caused the threshold reduction, including the accumulation of cavitation nuclei, the higher negative pressure at the antinodes, and the combined action of the multiple acoustic beams with different frequencies [32].

The third characteristic was the enhanced ADV process by the USW as illustrated in Fig. 4 . The cavitation pulses were applied to the phase-change emulsions. The pulse length, repetition frequency and peak negative pressure were 40 cycles, 1Hz, and 1.7MPa, respectively. The flow velocity was 6.4cm/s. Without the USW, the instants before and after the emission of one cavitation pulse were recorded in Fig.4(a) and (b); and no microbubble was found. In the USW field, only converged large bubbles could be observed at the nodes before the emission of a cavitation pulse in Fig.4(c); however, massive small microbubbles appeared between the nodes after the cavitation pulse was transmitted as shown in Fig.4(d). The small microbubbles between the two nodes of the USW attracted each other because of the second ARF and converged into large bubbles because of the cavitation pulses. The converged large bubbles would assemble at the nodes as shown in Fig.4(c). The pressure threshold of ADV should not be higher than the pressure threshold of phase-change microbubble erosion. Thus, the pressure threshold of ADV was not higher than 1.5MPa in the USW field, but was more than 1.7MPa without the USW. The USW reduced not only the pressure threshold of cavitation erosion but also the pressure threshold of the ADV process. The 3000Hz high-speed photography was used to capture the ADV processes, and the instants were chosen by slight vibration of the vessel wall. The duration of the observation was five seconds. The experiments were repeated three times. The probability of the occurrence of the ADV processes in the USW field was 87%. However, no ADV process happened without the USW. This maybe another reason for the threshold reduction of the phase-change microbubble erosion.

The two kinds of erosions, SonoVue microbubble erosion and phase-change microbubble erosion, were both antinodal superficial erosions, with pressure thresholds decreased in the USW field. However, their damage directions were different, the SonoVue microbubble erosion was in the distal vessel wall, while the phase-change microbubble erosion was in the proximal vessel. Thus, the damage of proximal wall or distal wall could be designated by choosing the erosion types.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Position shift of vessel-wall cavitation erosion
ce:section.ce:para: The positions of the vessel-wall cavitation erosions could be shifted without moving the transducers as presented in Fig. 5 . The centre positions of cavitation erosions were at the pressure antinodes. According to Eq. (4), the antinodes could be shifted via the relative phase change of the acoustic waves, which formed the USW. Thus, the position shifts of cavitation erosions were achieved by modulating the positions of the antinodes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Position shift of SonoVue microbubble erosion
ce:section.ce:para: The position of the SonoVue microbubble erosion could be shifted as shown in Fig.5(a) and (b). The original position of SonoVue microbubble erosion was in Fig.5(a). The position of SonoVue cavitation erosion in the distal vessel wall shifted after the USW modulation as observed in Fig.5(b). The relative phase change was 0.9π in the USW modulation. The average distance of three erosion shifts was 386.1 micrometres, which was very close to the theoretical simulation of 389.7 micrometres in Fig.5(e). The shift was easier to be observed with small erosion size. To generate the erosion of small size, the pulse peak negative pressure was set from 1.8MPa to 1.2MPa, while the values of other acoustic parameters and flow velocity were kept the same as in Section 3.1.1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Position shift of phase-change microbubble erosion
ce:section.ce:para: Fig.5(c) and (d) shows the position shift of the phase-change microbubble erosion. The original position of phase-change microbubble erosion was shown in Fig.5(c). After modulating the USW according to Eq. (4), the position of vessel-wall cavitation erosion moved following the antinodes as illustrated in Fig.5(d). The erosion holes would be fully filled by gas after a few minutes of the cavitation erosion. Consequently, the erosion holes were larger in Fig.5(d) than that in Fig.5(c). The relative phase change was 0.9π in the USW modulation. The phase-change microbubble erosion shifted 285.2 micrometres on average with a standard deviation of 18.7. The shift distance was slightly shorter than the simulated theoretical value in Fig.5(e). Except for the pulse length, which was set to 10 cycles, all the other experimental conditions were set to be the same as in Section 3.1.2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of cavitation pulse parameters on vessel-wall erosion
ce:section.ce:para: The impacts of three cavitation pulse parameters on cavitation erosion were explored, including pulse length, pulse repetition frequency, and peak negative pressure. By comparing these experimental results, the best parameters for precise superficial erosion could be chosen. In the following sections, the effects of cavitation pulse parameters on SonoVue microbubble erosion and on phase-change microbubble erosion are discussed separately. The experiments were repeated three times. The duration of cavitation erosion was 150s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of cavitation pulse parameters on SonoVue microbubble erosion
ce:section.ce:para: Three indicators were used to evaluate the SonoVue microbubble erosion: erosion length, erosion depth, and effective length. The length and the width of the minimum bounding rectangle of the whole erosion area were the erosion length and erosion depth. Because the SonoVue microbubble erosion arose between several adjacent nodes of the USW, the effective length was the sum of the erosion lengths at every pressure antinodes. For example, the erosion length and the erosion depth were the length and the width of the blue rectangle in Fig.6 (a), and the effective length was the sum of the lengths of the white arrows. The influences of pulse length, pulse repetition frequency, and peak negative pressure on SonoVue microbubble erosion would be evaluated by using the three indicators.

Fig. 6 shows the influences of pulse length on SonoVue microbubble erosion. The pulse lengths in Fig.6(a)–(c) were 2, 5, and 8 cycles. The pulse repetition frequency was 100Hz, and the peak negative pressure was 1.8MPa. The flow velocity was 6.4cm/s. The hollow erosion formed in Fig.6(a), but more tunnels appeared in Fig.6(b) and (c). The three indicators were measured after three repeated experiments. After radiation by cavitation pulses of different pulse lengths, the average erosion lengths were 2.31mm, 2.53mm, and 2.85mm, the average erosion depths were 0.15mm, 0.38mm, and 0.39mm, and the average effective lengths were 1.44mm, 1.60mm, and 1.69mm. The erosion depth increased more obviously than erosion length and the effective length if the pulse length increased as illustrated in Fig.6(d). The erosions of large depths may lead to arterial rupture and haemorrhage in vivo. Thus, the pulse length was the crucial parameter in SonoVue microbubble erosion, and a short pulse length of 2 cycles was more suitable for superficial erosion.

The effects of pulse repetition frequency on SonoVue microbubble erosion were indicated in Fig.6(e). The pulse repetition frequencies were 40Hz, 100Hz, and 160Hz. The pulse length was 2 cycles, and the peak negative pressure was 1.8MPa. The flow velocity was 6.4cm/s. The average erosion lengths under pulse repetition frequencies of 40Hz, 100Hz, and 160Hz were 1.62mm, 2.31mm, and 2.79mm, respectively, the average erosion depths were 0.03mm, 0.15mm, and 0.19mm, respectively, and the average effective lengths were 0.33mm, 1.44mm, and 2.08mm, respectively. The erosion length, the erosion depth, and the effective length increased significantly combined with the pulse repetition frequency. The erosion length and the erosion depth of 40Hz pulses were minimums, but the ratio of effective length to erosion length was too small, which meant that several very small damaged areas scattered at several antinodes. Hence, the reduction of pulse repetition frequency was not the primary choice to reduce the size of SonoVue microbubble erosion.

The impacts of pulse peak negative pressure on SonoVue microbubble erosion were illustrated in Fig.6(f). The peak negative pressures were 1.2MPa, 1.5MPa, and 1.8MPa. The pulse length was 2 cycles, and the pulse repetition frequency was 100Hz. The flow velocity was 6.4cm/s. By using the 1.2MPa, 1.5MPa, and 1.8MPa cavitation pulses, the average erosion lengths achieved 1.08mm, 1.88mm, and 2.31mm, respectively, the average erosion depths were 0.08mm, 0.10mm, and 0.15mm, respectively, and the average effective lengths were 0.73mm, 1.02mm, and 1.44mm, respectively. All three indicators increased if the peak negative pressure increased. A reduction in the pulse pressure could generate a smaller erosion with a smaller erosion depth. Because the erosion depths were still small with all of the peak negative pressures in Fig.6(f), the peak negative pressure could be chosen to control the erosion size.

Based on the comparison of the experimental results of the SonoVue microbubble erosion, pulse length was the crucial factor in cavitation pulses. A short pulse length was more appropriate for superficial erosion. A reduction of the pulse repetition frequency would not reduce the erosion length effectively, resulting in scattered small-sized SonoVue microbubble erosion. On the contrary, the reduction of peak negative pressure would reduce the erosion length significantly. Thus, the size of superficial erosion in the distal vessel wall could be controlled by setting the peak negative pressure of the cavitation pulses with a short pulse length.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of cavitation pulse parameters on phase-change microbubble erosion
ce:section.ce:para: Two indicators, erosion length and erosion depth, were used for the evaluation of phase-change microbubble erosion. The phase-change erosion could be restricted between two adjacent nodes in the USW field. The erosion length and depth were the length and the width of the smallest rectangle that could encircle the entire erosion area. The impacts of pulse length, pulse repetition frequency, and pulse peak negative pressure on phase-change microbubble erosion were studied based on the two indicators. The phase-change microbubble erosion experiments, with durations of 150s, were repeated three times.

Fig. 7 shows the effects of the length of cavitation pulses on phase-change microbubble erosion. The pulse lengths in Fig.7(a)–(c) were 10 cycles, 25 cycles and 40 cycles respectively. The pulse repeated frequency was 1Hz, and the peak negative pressure was 1.7MPa. The flow velocity was 6.4cm/s. After the exposures in the cavitation pulses of 10 cycles, 25 cycles, and 40 cycles, the average erosion lengths that were achieved were 0.15mm, 0.28mm, and 0.40mm, respectively, and the average erosion depths were 0.14mm, 0.16mm, and 0.25mm, respectively. Both the erosion length and erosion depths increased, while the erosion length increased more significantly than erosion depth as shown in Fig.7(d). The pulse length was the crucial factor for phase-change microbubble erosion, which had a significant impact on the erosion length.

The influences of pulse repetition frequency on phase-change microbubble erosion were illustrated in Fig.7(e). The pulse length was 10 cycles, and the peak negative pressure was 1.7MPa. The flow velocity was 6.4cm/s. The average erosion lengths under pulse repetition frequencies of 1Hz, 2.5Hz, and 4Hz were 0.15mm, 0.18mm, and 0.26mm, respectively, while the average erosion depths were 0.14mm, 0.15mm, and 0.19mm, respectively. Though the erosion length and the erosion depth increased by applying cavitation pulses with a higher repetition frequency, the increment was smaller compared to that in Fig.7(d), in which the pulse length increased.

The impacts of pulse peak negative pressure on phase-change microbubble erosion were indicated in Fig.7(f). The pulse length was 40 cycles, and the pulse repetition frequency was 1Hz. The flow velocity was 6.4cm/s. By using the 1.3MPa, 1.5MPa, and 1.7MPa cavitation pulses, the average erosion lengths that were achieved were 0.10mm, 0.23mm, and 0.40mm, respectively, and the average erosion depths were 0.11mm, 0.14mm, and 0.25mm, respectively. The two indicators, erosion length and erosion depth, were significantly increased if the peak negative pressure increased. The negative pressure was the other crucial cavitation pulse parameter for phase-change microbubble erosion. The desired erosion size could be controlled by setting the peak negative pressure as well.

There were some conclusions about the effects of cavitation pulse parameters by comparing these experimental results of the phase-change microbubble erosion. The pulse length could determine the size of the erosion, especially the erosion length. The peak negative pressure was the other crucial parameter, which significantly affected both erosion length and erosion depth. On the contrary, the pulse repetition frequency had a smaller impact on the erosion. Hence, adjusting pulse length and peak negative pressure under a low pulse repetition frequency could erode the proximal vessel wall with the desired erosion length and depth.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of flow velocity on vessel-wall cavitation erosion
ce:section.ce:para: The suspension flow velocity had a considerable impact on the efficiency of USW for cavitation nucleus collection. In the following sections, the effects of flow velocity on SonoVue microbubble erosion and on phase-change microbubble erosion are discussed separately. The experiments were repeated three times. The duration of the cavitation erosion was 150s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of flow velocity on SonoVue microbubble erosion
ce:section.ce:para: The relationships between the size of SonoVue microbubble erosion and the flow velocity of the suspension were presented in Fig. 8 . The pulse length was 2 cycles, and the pulse repetition frequency was 100Hz. The peak negative pressure of the cavitation pulses was 1.8MPa. The flow velocities of the suspensions were 6.4cm/s, 8.5cm/s, and 10.6cm/s. The corresponding numerical average erosion lengths achieved 2.31mm, 2.05mm, and 1.09mm, the average erosion depths were 0.15mm, 0.10mm, and 0.07mm, and the average effective lengths were 1.44mm, 1.11mm, and 0.54mm. SonoVue microbubble cavitation erosion was significantly affected by the flow velocity. The cavitation erosion size was greatly reduced when the flow velocity increased. Therefore, if flow velocity increased, there should be an increment in the acoustic pressures of cavitation pulses or the acoustic pressure of the USW to generate an erosion of the same size.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of flow velocity on phase-change microbubble erosion
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of flow velocity on phase-change microbubble erosion were shown in Fig. 9 . The pulse length was 40 cycles, the pulse repetition frequency was 1Hz, and the pulse peak negative pressure was 1.7MPa. The average erosion lengths were 0.40mm, 0.36mm, and 0.32mm, and the average erosion depths were 0.25mm, 0.24mm, and 0.19mm under flow velocities of 6.4cm/s, 8.5cm/s, and 10.6cm/s, respectively. When the flow velocity increased, the erosion size produced by phase-change microbubbles decreased. Nevertheless, compared to SonoVue microbubble erosion, flow velocity had a smaller impact on phase-change microbubble erosion.
This study presents an approach to control superficial vessel-wall erosions precisely by using USW, cavitation nuclei and medium-intensity cavitation pulses.

In comparison with the methods that only use travelling waves, the spatial controllability of cavitation erosion was improved in this study. First, the vessel-wall cavitation erosions were restricted between adjacent pressure nodes in the USW field, and the erosions could be shifted along the vessel precisely by modulating the USW without moving the transducers. Second, the erosion of the proximal vessel wall and the distal vessel wall could be determined by choosing the type of cavitation nuclei and their corresponding cavitation pulses. Third, the size of the erosion could be regulated by setting the parameters of the cavitation pulses. The controlled cavitation erosion could provide vessel damages on a submillimetre scale. Moreover, this approach was more suitable for superficial cavitation erosion in blood vessels. The controlled cavitation erosions extended superficially in the vessel’s axial direction. The depths of the erosions were less than 0.4mm, which is a greater improvement compared with the more than 1mm tunnels extending along the direction of sound propagation induced by travelling waves [11].

Again, in contrast with mechanical arterial balloon injury, the cavitation erosion controlled by an USW could cause endothelial cell damage in determined areas at predictable sites, and this noninvasive approach could avoid excessive vessel injuries. In summary, this approach can implement accurate superficial vessel damage, and shows potential for inducement of predictable atherosclerosis in animal models.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge Jing Li and Qi Chen for helpful discussions, and Hong Hu and Shanshan Xu for contributions to the experimental setup. This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 81127901 and 11474229).
